title: tethys: fuzzy fallback for --package suggestions (edit distance)
description:
tethys coupling --package <name> uses substring match (case-insensitive) for the "Did you mean:" suggestions when the name isn't found. A user who types rivets_mcp (underscore) for rivets-mcp (hyphen), or rivetsmcp (no separator), gets no suggestion. cargo's own suggestion logic uses Levenshtein-distance fallback in this case.

Reviewer flagged this as "known limitation, could be quality-of-life follow-up" across multiple PR 60 review rounds.

Scope: extend collect_suggestions in crates/tethys/src/cli/coupling.rs to fall back to edit-distance ranking when substring match yields nothing. Use the strsim crate (already in the rust ecosystem) or a hand-rolled DP impl.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Substring match still used first (preserves current behavior for partial matches)
- [ ] Edit-distance fallback when substring yields zero results
- [ ] Configurable distance threshold (default ~3)
- [ ] Unit tests covering: snake-vs-kebab mismatch, missing separator, single-char typo, very distant name yields no suggestion

title: Type hierarchy: extract and traverse extends/implements edges
description:
Add type-hierarchy queries — walk inheritance and trait/interface implementation chains upward (base types) or downward (derived types).

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph_type_hierarchy tool.

**Languages:**
- Rust: impl X for Y, trait X: Y + Z, struct X<T: Bound>
- C#: class X : BaseClass, IInterface, IGeneric<T>

**Schema additions:**
- New ReferenceKind variants: Extends, Implements (already partly modeled in ReferenceKind enum — check current state).
- Tree-sitter extractors for both languages emit these edges during indexing.
- Reuse the existing refs table; no new tables needed.

**Why this depends on rivets-aay4:**
For methods, we want to walk ''method M of class X'' to its overridden version in BaseClass. That requires knowing which class a method belongs to, which is parent_symbol_id. Without it, we can do class-to-class hierarchy but not method-to-method override resolution.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::get_type_hierarchy(symbol: &str, direction: HierarchyDirection) -> Result<TypeHierarchy>
- enum HierarchyDirection { Up, Down, Both }

**CLI:**
- tethys hierarchy <SYMBOL> [--direction up|down|both]
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Rust extractor emits Extends/Implements edges for impl blocks and trait bounds
- [ ] C# extractor emits Extends/Implements edges for class inheritance and interface lists
- [ ] get_type_hierarchy() API method
- [ ] CLI subcommand tethys hierarchy
- [ ] Tests on a Rust trait fixture and a C# class-hierarchy fixture
- [ ] MCP tool tethys_type_hierarchy (sibling rivets-o4re)

title: Surface architecture phase stats in tethys index output
description:
The architecture phase populates IndexStats.architecture with packages_recorded/files_assigned/package_deps_recorded counts, but crates/tethys/src/cli/index.rs never displays them. Users running 'tethys index' see 'Indexed 42 files' but no indication that the architecture phase ran or how many packages were detected.

**Fix:** Add a section to the index command output that conditionally prints architecture counts when stats.architecture is Some. Mirror the existing 'Files', 'Symbols', 'References' section style.

Found during final review of rivets-byie.

title: unused-imports/parser: nested-group 'use crate::{util::Helper}' drops intermediate path segment
description:
A nested-group import such as `use crate::{util::Helper};` (or
`use crate::{db::{self}, util::Helper}`) loses its intermediate path segment.
`tethys unused-imports` then reports the wrong `source_module` — "crate" instead
of "crate::util" — in both `--json` and human output, and downgrades confidence
to `MaybeTrait` even for a workspace struct.

Reproduced via probe during review:
- `src/util.rs`: `pub struct Helper;`
- `src/lib.rs`: `use crate::{util::Helper};` (Helper never used)
- Result: finding `name="Helper", source_module="crate", confidence=MaybeTrait`.

Root cause: `collect_use_list_names` (src/languages/rust.rs) keeps only the
FINAL segment of a nested `scoped_identifier` list member (it calls
`parse_scoped_identifier` and takes just the name), discarding the intermediate
`util`. `parse_scoped_use_list` fills `path` only from the OUTER list's path
child. So `import.path` collapses to `["crate"]` and `imported_names` to
`["Helper"]`.

The deeper cause is the `ImportStatement` model itself: it carries ONE
`path: Vec<String>` for MANY `imported_names: Vec<String>`, so it structurally
cannot represent `use a::{b::C, d::E}` where each name has a distinct path.

Impact beyond display: because `import.path` collapses to `["crate"]`,
downstream module resolution (`classify_confidence`, and in principle
`compute_dependencies`) resolves the wrong module — compounding with the
`resolve_crate_path([])` gap tracked in the sibling issue.

Direction: conservative for unused-imports (hides/downgrades), but the wrong
`source_module` string is user-visible in the report.

Source: /code-review max multi-agent review of the unused-imports PR
(PR #4, branch `index-correctness`) — finding #2.
design:
This is a model change and should be scoped as a design step first (it ripples
to every import consumer), which is why it is its own PR.

Two viable shapes:

1. Expand nested groups at parse time into multiple `ImportStatement`s, one per
   leaf, each carrying its full path (`use crate::{util::Helper, db::Conn}` ->
   two statements: path `["crate","util"]` name `Helper`, and path
   `["crate","db"]` name `Conn`). Least invasive for consumers — each keeps
   assuming one path per statement.

2. Give the model a per-name path (each imported name resolves against its own
   segment list). More expressive but touches every reader.

Touches: `ImportStatement` (src/languages/common.rs), the Rust use-parser
(`collect_use_list_names`, `parse_scoped_use_list`, `parse_use_declaration`),
and every consumer of `path` + `imported_names` (`compute_dependencies`,
`unused_imports`, the C# `UsingDirective` converter). Prefer option 1 unless a
consumer genuinely needs the grouped form.

Note: fixing this narrows but does not fully close the crate-root case — the
resolver hole in the sibling issue (`resolve_crate_path([])` -> src/ dir) must
also land for `use crate::Foo;` to reach Definite.
acceptance_criteria:
* `use crate::{util::Helper};` (unused) reports source_module "crate::util"
  and, with the index present, confidence Definite (given the sibling
  resolver fix).
* Heterogeneous nested groups (`use a::{b::C, d::E}`) resolve each imported
  name against its own full path.
* Parser tests cover nested-group path retention; full suite green.
* No behavior change for flat imports and single-name statements.

title: Compute content hash for indexed files
description:
The schema supports `content_hash` but it's always `None`. See `batch_writer.rs:238`, `lib.rs:643`, `lib.rs:803`.

Content hashing would provide more reliable change detection than mtime/size alone, useful for:
- Detecting changes after file touch without content change
- Cache key generation
- Detecting identical content in different files

title: Add lsp-types dependency
description:
Add `lsp-types = "0.97"` to Cargo.toml for LSP protocol types.

This provides all the types we need: InitializeParams, GotoDefinitionParams, Location, etc.

title: Expand language support: Python, TypeScript, Go
description:
Add language extractors for the three highest-leverage languages beyond Rust and C#. KiroGraph supports 17 languages; tethys can borrow the same tree-sitter grammars.

**Why these three first:**
- Python: huge ecosystem; tree-sitter-python is mature; many Rust crates wrap Python.
- TypeScript: covers frontend; necessary for full-stack repos and any project using Node tooling. Grammar is tree-sitter-typescript (also covers .tsx via tree-sitter-tsx).
- Go: well-defined module system; grammar is small and stable; popular in infrastructure code.

**Approach:**
Tethys's languages/ module is already structured around per-language extractors (common.rs, csharp.rs, rust.rs, tree_sitter_utils.rs). Each new language gets its own file plus grammar dependency in Cargo.toml.

Each extractor needs to handle:
- Symbol extraction (functions, classes/structs, methods, types)
- Visibility (Python lacks formal modifiers; convention is leading underscore)
- Imports / module resolution (Python: import / from; TS: import { } from; Go: import "path")
- Test detection (Python: pytest test_*, unittest.TestCase; TS: describe/it; Go: func TestXxx)
- Reference extraction (call expressions, type annotations)

**Dependencies to add:**
- tree-sitter-python = ''0.23''
- tree-sitter-typescript = ''0.23''
- tree-sitter-go = ''0.23''

**Suggested split:**
This issue is an epic — break into one child issue per language so they can ship incrementally:
- Python language support
- TypeScript language support
- Go language support

Each child should add: grammar dep, extractor module, common test fixture under crates/tethys/tests/fixtures/<lang>/, and integration tests. The tree-sitter parsing infrastructure is already done — adding a language is mostly a query-pattern translation exercise.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Three child issues created (Python, TypeScript, Go), each independently shippable
- [ ] Each language reaches feature parity with the Rust extractor: symbols, refs, imports, test detection
- [ ] Integration test on a representative open-source repo per language
- [ ] tethys index detects language correctly by file extension
- [ ] README updated with new language list

title: Add imports table to schema
Untested-code analysis: symbols unreachable from any test root
description:
## Parent

tethys-l6nt (scope PRD) — Act 1 roadmap, second stage. Deliberately precedes dead code: same reverse-traversal machinery, cheaper failure mode (a wrong untested claim wastes a test run; a wrong dead claim deletes live code).

## What to build

An analysis reporting symbols that no test can reach: take is_test symbols as roots (test detection already exists), traverse the reference graph through callees, and report indexed symbols outside the covered set. Reuses the existing reachability machinery; CLI subcommand with table and JSON output.

Two decisions from the PRD bind here:
- Traversal must consume the refs table, not only call_edges — call-edge population skips references that occur outside any enclosing symbol, and the analysis must not inherit that blind spot.
- Known limitation to document rather than solve here: functions used only as values (callbacks) have no inbound refs until tethys-ygjx lands, so callback-tested code can appear untested. Related, not blocking — the failure mode is acceptable for this stage.

## Blocked by

None - can start immediately (related: tethys-ygjx).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: a function called (transitively) from a #[test] function is excluded from the report; a function no test reaches is included
- [ ] Traversal consumes refs including top-level references — a fixture pins a case that call_edges alone would miss
- [ ] CLI subcommand with table and JSON output
- [ ] Known-limitations section documents the fn-as-value gap with a pointer to tethys-ygjx
- [ ] Works for both Rust and C# test roots (is_test covers both)
- [ ] Tests build their own index from fixture workspaces, never an ambient DB

title: Nested C# namespace blocks don't participate in namespace resolution
description: namespace Outer1 { namespace Inner1 { ... } } stores two separate un-dotted Module symbols (Outer1, Inner1) with NO parent_symbol_id chain (probed 2026-06-06, .csharp-ns/probe-findings.md), so the namespace map (keyed on Module symbol names) can never match a `using Outer1.Inner1;` directive. Pre-existing limitation — the deleted resolve_csharp_dependencies post-pass had the same gap. Fix requires the C# extractor to either parent-chain nested namespace modules or store the dotted name; then the map builder reconstructs keys. Modern C# rarely uses nested blocks (dotted declarations and file-scoped namespaces both work today), hence deferred. Surfaced during the jwf9 loop design (.csharp-ns/design.md claim C2).

title: Reachability analysis (forward/backward data flow)
notes: Primitives already exist - just needs BFS wrapper. Removed dependency on call_edges table since we can use existing get_callers/get_callees.

title: tethys: C# parser enhancements — unsafe modifier and type parameters
description: The C# function signature extraction is missing: (1) detection of the unsafe modifier (is_unsafe always false), (2) extraction of generic type parameters (generics always None).
notes: Closed: Duplicate of tethys-itez (same two TODO sites: unsafe modifier + generics extraction, now at src/languages/csharp.rs:1030,1032).

title: use-list alias members dropped: use m::{A, B as C} loses B from imports and refs
description:
collect_use_list_names (Rust use-declaration parsing) handles identifier and scoped_identifier children of a use_list but has NO use_as_clause arm, so a group member with an alias — use m::{A, B as C} — drops B entirely: no imports row, and (post tethys-v1w8) no reexport ref when the statement is pub use.

Impact today: the import of B is invisible to unused-imports, file_deps corroboration, and Pass-2 explicit-import resolution (a body use of C resolves how?  — verify while fixing). Impact post-v1w8: a group-aliased re-export target keeps zero inbound refs (dead-code false positive persists for exactly this shape).

Top-level alias (use m::B as C;) is handled correctly by parse_use_as_clause — this is only the in-group form.

Sibling of tethys-pdea (nested-group intermediate segment drop) — same collect_use_list_names neighborhood, distinct symptom (pdea keeps the name with a wrong path; this drops the name entirely).

Discovered during tethys-v1w8 slice 2 while establishing the parity baseline for reexport-ref emission; v1w8 deliberately mirrors current parse behavior rather than fixing this (parity posture).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] use m::{A, B as C} yields an imports row for B (alias C recorded)
- [ ] pub use m::{A, B as C} yields a reexport ref for B (original name, per v1w8 C3)
- [ ] Nested and multi-alias groups covered by fixture
- [ ] Existing non-alias group parsing unchanged (regression)

title: Pre-computed call graph edges table
description:
Store explicit caller→callee edges in a dedicated table for faster graph queries.

**Current approach:**
- `get_callers` joins references → symbols → files
- Complex query with multiple joins
- Re-computed on every query

**Drift's approach:**
- Dedicated `call_edges` table: `(caller_id, callee_id, call_count)`
- Pre-computed during indexing
- Simple lookups for callers/callees

**Proposed schema:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE call_edges (
    caller_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    callee_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    call_count INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
    PRIMARY KEY (caller_symbol_id, callee_symbol_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (caller_symbol_id) REFERENCES symbols(id),
    FOREIGN KEY (callee_symbol_id) REFERENCES symbols(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_call_edges_callee ON call_edges(callee_symbol_id);
```

**Benefits:**
- O(1) lookup for "who calls X?" instead of join-heavy query
- Enables efficient reachability analysis
- Pre-computed call counts for hotspot detection

**Trade-off:**
- Slightly larger database
- Need to update edges during incremental re-index

title: MCP server for tethys tools
description:
Expose tethys functionality as MCP tools for AI agent integration. AI assistants like Claude/Cursor/Copilot can then query the indexed graph instead of grepping files, dramatically cutting tool-call count and context usage on long sessions.

Inspired by both Drift (50+ MCP tools across 7 layers) and KiroGraph (16 MCP tools, all auto-approved). KiroGraph's design observation drives most of the value: ''Kiro literally queries the graph instead of grepping files,'' so the index becomes context the model uses for every prompt.

**MVP tools (wrap existing Tethys API methods):**

| Tool | Description | Underlying API |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| tethys_status | Index stats, language breakdown | get_stats |
| tethys_symbol | Get symbol by qualified name | get_symbol |
| tethys_search | Search symbols by name/kind | search_symbols |
| tethys_callers | Who calls this symbol? | get_callers |
| tethys_callees | What does this symbol call? | get_symbol_dependencies |
| tethys_impact | Impact analysis for a symbol | get_symbol_impact |
| tethys_file_impact | Impact analysis for a file | get_impact |
| tethys_reachable | Forward/backward reachability | get_forward_reachable / get_backward_reachable |
| tethys_cycles | Detect dependency cycles | detect_cycles |
| tethys_panic_points | Find .unwrap()/.expect() calls | get_panic_points |
| tethys_affected_tests | Tests affected by changed files | get_affected_tests |

**v2 tools (depend on new analytics — see sibling issues):**

| Tool | Description | Depends on |
|------|-------------|------------|
| tethys_path | Shortest path between two symbols | new path-query feature |
| tethys_dead_code | Symbols with zero incoming refs | new dead-code feature |
| tethys_hotspots | Most-connected symbols | new hotspots feature |
| tethys_surprising | Non-obvious cross-file edges | new surprising-connections feature |
| tethys_type_hierarchy | Up/down extends-implements walk | new type-hierarchy feature |
| tethys_coupling | Per-package Ca/Ce/instability | new architecture-metrics feature |
| tethys_diff | Graph diff vs. saved snapshot | new snapshot/diff feature |

**Implementation plan:**
- New crates/tethys-mcp crate (binary + thin lib).
- Use rmcp (already in workspace via rivets-mcp).
- Each tool is a thin async wrapper around a Tethys method.
- Tools should default to auto-approved in the Claude MCP config so the model can call them freely.
- Coexists with rivets-mcp on a single MCP host.

**Benefits:**
- AI agents query codebase structure instead of grepping files.
- Integrates with Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, and any MCP-compatible client.
- Complements rivets-mcp (issue tracker) and tethys's CLI.

title: tethys: implement depth-limited transitive impact analysis
description: The --depth flag in impact analysis is accepted but not implemented. Currently, transitive analysis always explores the full dependency graph. Implement depth limiting to control how many levels of indirection to follow.
notes: Closed: Already implemented: --depth was wired through tethys impact in PR #57 (verified at src/cli/impact.rs:16,24 — depth passed to get_symbol_impact/get_impact). Remaining follow-ups tracked in tethys-w0qw (--depth 0 semantics) and tethys-3yxn (callers --transitive parity).

title: refs table omits bare free-function call references (only method & qualified calls recorded)
description:
Tethys does not record references to bare free-function calls (e.g. make_service(), validate_kiro_project_path()) in the refs table. Method calls (.unwrap(), .expect()) and path-qualified calls (PathBuf::from, InstallMode::from) ARE recorded, so the gap is specifically unqualified free-function invocations.

This makes callers, impact, reachability, and any SQL-over-refs analysis silently incomplete for free functions: a free function appears to have zero call-site references even when it is called throughout the codebase.

Discovered while designing a plan-lint gate (kiro-control-center kiro-6g6r) that intended to detect make_service() calls via refs.reference_name. The refs half was vacuous; we fell back to the imports table (which is complete and accurate). The gate works without this, but the underlying indexer gap is real and affects caller/impact correctness.
design:
Repro (tethys 0.1.0, fresh index of crates/kiro-control-center):

  tethys --workspace . index    # 74 files, 1815 symbols, 20235 refs
  DB=.rivets/index/tethys.db

  # Bare free-fn calls -> 0 refs, despite being called in every command wrapper:
  sqlite3 $DB "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM refs WHERE reference_name='make_service';"               # 0
  sqlite3 $DB "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM refs WHERE reference_name='validate_kiro_project_path';" # 0

  # Contrast - these DO work:
  sqlite3 $DB "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM refs WHERE reference_name IN ('unwrap','expect');"        # 5361
  sqlite3 $DB "SELECT DISTINCT reference_name FROM refs WHERE reference_name LIKE '%::from';" # PathBuf::from, InstallMode::from, ...

  # Ground truth: validate_kiro_project_path is called in 6+ command files (ripgrep confirms).

Likely cause: the tree-sitter-rust reference extractor captures call_expression whose function is a field_expression (method call) or scoped_identifier (A::b), but not a plain identifier (bare free-fn call). Suggest extending ref extraction to record call_expression with a bare identifier callee, with reference_name = the identifier and kind = 'call'.
acceptance_criteria:
* After indexing a workspace containing a bare free-function call foo() (function defined in-crate, called without qualification), SELECT COUNT(*) FROM refs WHERE reference_name='foo' returns >= 1.
* tethys callers <free_fn> lists the bare-call sites.
* Existing method-call and qualified-call ref extraction is unchanged (regression test).
* A unit/integration fixture pins a bare free-fn call producing a refs row.
notes:
INVALID — closed not-a-bug (2026-06-30, verified against code + live self-index).

The stated root cause is wrong: src/languages/rust.rs:271 has an IDENTIFIER arm in extract_call_reference that emits a Call ref for bare foo() — present since the Phase-2 commit (303f2db), well before this was filed. Bare free-function calls ARE captured, stored, resolved, and present in both refs and call_edges.

The repro measured the wrong column. Resolution NULLs reference_name (src/db/references.rs:157: UPDATE refs SET symbol_id=?2, reference_name=NULL), and initial storage only sets reference_name when symbol_id IS NULL. So reference_name is populated ONLY for UNRESOLVED refs (std method calls like unwrap/expect). make_service resolved in-crate => reference_name NULL => the WHERE reference_name='make_service' query is structurally empty.

Proof (self-index): bare free fn node_text => 0 rows WHERE reference_name='node_text', but 60 refs + 37 call_edges via JOIN symbols on symbol_id. Dead-code finder would NOT flag it.

The genuine issue behind the symptom is the reference_name nulling itself (refs not name-queryable for resolved symbols) — filed separately. The fn-as-value / macro-token gap is tracked in tethys-ygjx.

Closed: Not a bug: bare free-fn calls are captured (verified). Repro queried reference_name, which resolution NULLs for resolved in-crate symbols. Superseded by the reference_name-nulling issue (see notes).

title: BatchWriter error-handling hardening: surface failures instead of log-and-continue (consolidates 3 issues)
description:
Consolidated design decision: how does BatchWriter surface failure? Three previously separate tickets each covered one facet and risked three inconsistent answers if fixed independently. Bundles tethys-24av, tethys-ghip, tethys-kivr (all closed as consolidated-into-this; full details remain readable there via rivets show).

Facets:
1. (was tethys-24av) WriteStats only counts successes. Add failed_files (path + error message) so failures survive into the final result instead of being lost after a log line.
2. (was tethys-ghip) write_batch() catches ALL database errors as warnings and continues — systemic failures (disk full, DB corruption) produce thousands of warnings instead of one clear abort. Add a consecutive-failure threshold (e.g. MAX_CONSECUTIVE_FAILURES = 5) that converts systemic failure into a hard error.
3. (was tethys-kivr) BatchWriter::send() silently drops data when the channel is disconnected (background writer thread crashed), only logging. Return Result so callers can detect data loss.

These should be designed together: the threshold (2) feeds the stats (1), and the send() contract (3) determines who observes the abort. One PR, one error-propagation story.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] WriteStats carries failed_files with per-file error context
- [ ] Consecutive-failure threshold aborts write_batch on systemic errors with a single clear error
- [ ] send() returns Result; callers handle or propagate channel disconnect
- [ ] Tests: injected write failure appears in WriteStats; threshold aborts; disconnected channel surfaces as Err
- [ ] Error-propagation behavior documented on BatchWriter

title: Implement parent_symbol_id for nested symbol tracking
description: Two TODO sites in lib.rs (lines 691, 823) pass None for parent_symbol_id. Implementing this would enable tracking nested symbols (methods within impl blocks, nested functions) for more precise dependency graphs.
notes: Closed: Duplicate of rivets-aay4 (older, describes the same parent_symbol_id TODOs in lib.rs).

title: Implement incremental index updates
description:
Currently `update()` in `lib.rs:2095-2104` re-indexes everything. The schema stores `mtime_ns` and `size_bytes` which could enable proper incremental updates:

1. Query files from DB
2. Compare mtime/size with filesystem
3. Only re-parse changed files
4. Remove deleted files from index

This would significantly improve performance for large codebases.
notes: Design constraint (from codebase-memory-mcp comparison, 2026-07-01): incremental via demote-and-rerun-Pass-2 is currently LOSSY. Pass 2 NULLs refs.reference_name on resolution (tethys-6rlu). When a changed file's symbols are deleted, inbound resolved refs from OTHER files must be demoted to unresolved and re-resolved — the bare name is recoverable from the symbol row inside the same transaction BEFORE the delete, but refs that originally carried a qualified path (e.g. crate::helper) cannot be reconstructed from symbols.name. A scoped Pass-2 re-run would therefore see weaker input than the original full pass, and the required fence — incremental(edit) produces a canonical dump byte-identical to full-rebuild(edited tree), reusing the .idxperf/probe-dump.py oracle — would diverge. Any incremental design must either retain the original reference_name (separate column; do NOT resurrect denormalizing onto reference_name — the 6rlu subtractive sweep showed the NULL semantics are load-bearing for panic-points and streaming file_deps) or snapshot demotion info at delete time. Reference pattern: codebase-memory-mcp pipeline_incremental.c snapshots cross-file inbound edges before delete+reinsert, then re-links.

title: Dead-code finder: symbols with zero incoming references
description:
Find unexported / non-public symbols with no incoming references — candidates for removal. Trivial to implement on the existing schema; high developer-value signal.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph_dead_code tool and CLI command.

**Implementation:**
Single SQL query:
  SELECT s.* FROM symbols s
  LEFT JOIN call_edges c ON c.callee_symbol_id = s.id
  LEFT JOIN refs r ON r.symbol_id = s.id
  WHERE c.callee_symbol_id IS NULL
    AND r.symbol_id IS NULL
    AND s.visibility != 'public'
  LIMIT ?;

Filtering to non-public is important — public/exported symbols may be used by consumers outside the indexed workspace, so reporting them as dead would generate false positives.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::find_dead_code(limit: usize) -> Result<Vec<Symbol>>

**CLI:**
- tethys dead-code [--limit N] [--json]

**Caveats to document:**
- Macro-expanded references aren't tracked, so macro-only call sites can produce false positives.
- Trait-method dispatch through dyn Trait may not appear as a direct call edge; investigate before reporting.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] find_dead_code() API method
- [ ] CLI subcommand tethys dead-code with --limit and --json
- [ ] Filters out public/exported symbols
- [ ] Documents known false-positive sources in module docs
- [ ] MCP tool tethys_dead_code (sibling rivets-o4re)
- [ ] Tests on a fixture with both clearly-dead and clearly-live symbols

title: tethys: plumb CancellationToken through index_with_options
description:
Surfaced by silent-failure-hunter during PR #65 multi-agent review. Pre-existing gap exposed by the rivets-lcb6 fix, not a regression introduced by it.

index_with_options has NO cancellation plumbing today. Before rivets-lcb6, a user Ctrl-C mid-index left the DB with stale-but-valid file_deps from the prior run. After rivets-lcb6, the same Ctrl-C between line 140 (clear_all_file_deps) and the repopulate phases leaves file_deps EMPTY. From the user's perspective, cancellation is now strictly worse: downstream queries (get_file_dependencies, impact analysis) return [] until the next successful index.

Two issues are linked but distinct:
1. (this issue) Plumb a CancellationToken (or std::sync::atomic::AtomicBool / tokio::sync::CancellationToken) through index_with_options. Check it at well-defined yield points: after workspace discovery, after each batch_writer.finish(), before each clear_all_X.
2. (rivets-ml05) Wrap the clear+repopulate in a transaction so abort restores prior state.

Either approach mitigates the worst-case 'empty DB after Ctrl-C' UX. Doing both gives belt + suspenders: transaction handles panics and SQLite errors; cancellation handles user-initiated abort cleanly.

Suggested API surface:
- index_with_options(&self, options: IndexOptions) → unchanged caller-facing default; internally checks an Arc<AtomicBool>
- index_with_options_cancellable(&self, options: IndexOptions, cancel: &CancellationToken) → new variant for CLI / MCP callers

Source: PR #65 multi-agent review aggregate (silent-failure-hunter Suggestion #4).
acceptance_criteria:
* CancellationToken (or equivalent) accepted by indexing entry points
* Cancellation checks at: post-discovery, between batches, before each clear_all_X
* On cancel: structured warn! log noting which phase aborted
* Test: spawn an index, cancel mid-flight, assert clean error return (not panic) and DB state is consistent with rivets-ml05 (either fully prior-state or fully cleared)

title: Track failed files in WriteStats for debugging
description:
Currently `WriteStats` only tracks successful writes. When files fail to write, that information is logged but lost from the final result.

**Current struct:**
```rust
pub struct WriteStats {
    pub files_written: usize,
    pub symbols_written: usize,
    pub references_written: usize,
    pub batches_committed: usize,
}
```

**Suggested addition:**
```rust
pub struct WriteStats {
    // ... existing fields ...
    /// Files that failed to write with their error messages
    pub failed_files: Vec<(PathBuf, String)>,
}
```

**Benefits:**
- Users can see which files need attention without grepping logs
- Enables retry logic for failed files
- Makes partial success explicit rather than hidden

**Trade-off:** Increases memory usage if many files fail (unlikely in practice).
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-rddn (BatchWriter error-handling bundle); the three facets share one design decision and should ship as one PR.

title: Visibility-tightening analysis: public items that could be crate-private (Rust)
description:
## Parent

tethys-l6nt (scope PRD) — Act 1 roadmap, first stage (paired with deprecated-callers).

## What to build

An analysis listing items whose declared visibility exceeds their observed use: pub items referenced only from within their own crate are candidates for pub(crate). End-to-end: facade query over symbols + resolved refs, CLI subcommand with table and JSON output, findings carry the Definite/Maybe confidence tiers required by the PRD error posture (suppressions, not accusations).

Tiering decisions that bind here:
- A library crate's root public API may have consumers outside the indexed workspace, which tethys cannot see. Public items of published library crates therefore downgrade to Maybe by default; Definite is reserved for visibility surfaces the workspace fully encloses.
- Re-exports widen the effective visibility surface: an item that is pub-use re-exported must not be flagged just because its direct references are crate-local. This is why the re-export slice blocks this one.
- The fn-as-value refs gap (tethys-ygjx) can make a cross-crate callback-only use invisible. Document as a known limitation; conservative tiering absorbs it.

Self-index sanity check: run against tethys itself and review the findings once by hand — rustc has no lint for this, so there is no automatic oracle; the review documents the observed false-positive rate.

## Blocked by

- tethys-v1w8 (re-export references) — re-exported items would otherwise be false tightening candidates.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: a pub fn used only within its own crate is flagged; a pub fn used cross-crate in the workspace is not
- [ ] Fixture: a pub-use re-exported item is not flagged even when its direct refs are crate-local
- [ ] Published-library-root public items downgrade to Maybe tier by default
- [ ] CLI subcommand with table and JSON output; tier visible in both
- [ ] Known-limitations documented (fn-as-value gap, pointer to tethys-ygjx)
- [ ] One-time reviewed self-index run recorded in the PR (observed false-positive posture)
- [ ] Tests build their own index from fixture workspaces, never an ambient DB

title: Method calls resolve by name only: x.unwrap() on external types misattributed to a same-named in-crate method
description:
Method-call resolution matches on the method NAME without receiver-type information, so a call like x.unwrap() where x is an external/std type (e.g. Option) resolves to an in-crate method of the same name when one exists (e.g. an inherent Thing::unwrap).

Effects:
1. Fabricated call edge: an unrelated caller appears to call the in-crate Thing::unwrap (over-attribution in callers/impact/reachability).
2. Hidden external call: the genuine external .unwrap() gets a symbol_id and its reference_name is nulled, so panic-points (WHERE reference_name IN ('unwrap','expect')) reports 0 — a false negative.

Repro:
  src/lib.rs:
    pub struct Thing; impl Thing { pub fn unwrap(&self) {} }
    pub fn use_external() { let x: Option<i32> = Some(1); x.unwrap(); }
    pub fn use_internal()  { let t = Thing; t.unwrap(); }
  After index: BOTH unwrap call refs have the SAME symbol_id (the in-crate Thing::unwrap); Panic Points Analysis
===============================================================

title: Pass 3 LSP re-verifies speculative-band resolutions, not just unresolved refs
description:
Today Pass 3 LSP only fetches refs Pass 2 DECLINED (symbol_id IS NULL). Confidently-wrong Pass-2 bindings — the tethys-53iv phantom-edge class — are therefore structurally invisible to rust-analyzer, the one component with receiver-type information that could correct them.

Once resolution provenance bands exist (blocking epic), widen the Pass 3 candidate set from "unresolved" to "unresolved UNION speculative-band". Match-back is position-based (goto_definition at the ref location -> find_symbol_at_line), so this does NOT depend on reference_name retention.

Policy decisions to settle in this issue before implementation:
- LSP disagrees with the speculative binding -> rebind symbol_id and upgrade band to lsp.
- LSP confirms -> upgrade band in place (binding unchanged).
- LSP timeout/error -> leave the speculative binding untouched (counted and capped, as today; never demote on LSP failure).
- Scoping/volume: per-request timeout is 60s, so re-verifying every speculative ref may be slow on large crates. Likely restrict to method-call refs (the 53iv class) and/or cap per run. Decide and document.

Still opt-in behind --lsp; the default path is unchanged (provenance epic handles labeling; 53iv handles the default-path fix).

CBM analog from the 2026-07-01 comparison: their type-aware resolver arbitrates against the textual registry with a 0.6 confidence floor, CALLS edges only — i.e., the type-aware component corrects the textual one rather than merely filling its gaps. Ours is a real language server, so its verdicts deserve at least that much authority.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Override policy decided and documented (disagree->rebind+lsp band; confirm->upgrade in place; timeout/error->untouched)
- [ ] Volume scoping decided (method-call-only and/or per-run cap) and documented
- [ ] Pass 3 candidate query covers unresolved UNION speculative
- [ ] Fence: fixture where Pass 2 binds speculatively and rust-analyzer corrects it (requires real rust-analyzer, alongside lsp_resolution.rs)
- [ ] Default (no --lsp) behavior byte-identical to before

title: Implement Pass 2: resolve references against symbol DB
description:
After all files are indexed, run a second pass to resolve unresolved references:

1. Query all references with symbol_id = NULL
2. For each, look up import context from imports table
3. Match reference name against symbols from imported modules
4. Update reference with resolved symbol_id

Resolution priority:
1. Same-file lookup
2. Explicit import lookup
3. Qualified path resolution
4. Glob import search
5. Leave unresolved (LSP candidate)

title: tethys: rename format_uri to absolute_path_to_uri
description:
format_uri's rustdoc says 'caller must pass an absolute, canonicalized path' — that precondition lives only in prose. A CanonicalPath newtype would be overkill for two private callsites, but the rename converts a hidden precondition into a name a reviewer can't miss.

Trivial: rename the fn, update the one caller in path_to_uri, update the five format_uri_* test names if desired (or leave them as the shorter form).

If the function ever goes pub(crate), revisit with CanonicalPath. Defer until that day.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (type-design-analyzer).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Rename fn format_uri -> fn absolute_path_to_uri in crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs
- [ ] Update caller in path_to_uri
- [ ] Update doc comments referencing the old name
- [ ] cargo nextest run -p tethys passes
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-w3z7 (transport.rs format_uri hardening bundle); full details preserved here.

title: unused-imports: crate-root 'use crate::Foo;' downgraded to MaybeTrait (bare crate resolves to src/ dir)
description:
`tethys unused-imports` reports a genuinely-unused crate-root import such as
`use crate::Foo;` (where `Foo` is a non-trait workspace symbol defined at the
crate root, e.g. `pub struct Foo;` in `src/lib.rs`) as `MaybeTrait` — hidden by
default, shown only under `--all` — even though the index can prove `Foo` is a
struct and it should be `Definite`.

Reproduced via probe during review:
- `src/lib.rs`: `pub struct Foo;`
- `src/inner.rs`: `use crate::Foo;` (Foo never used)
- Result: finding `name="Foo", source_module="crate", confidence=MaybeTrait`.

Root cause is a resolver asymmetry, not a feature bug. `classify_confidence`
resolves `import.path == ["crate"]` via `resolve_import_segments` ->
`resolve_module_path(["crate"], …)` -> `resolve_crate_path(&[], crate_root)`
(src/resolver.rs:88-91), whose empty-path branch returns the crate-root
**directory**. `relative_path` -> "src"; `get_file_id("src")` -> None
(directories have no rows), so the symbol lookup is skipped and confidence falls
through to `MaybeTrait`.

The sibling workspace-crate arm handles the analogous bare-crate reference
correctly, returning `target.entry_point_file().filter(|p| p.exists())`
(src/resolver.rs:52-58). The `"crate" =>` self-arm does not — that is the gap.

Direction: conservative. The import is still correctly identified as unused;
only its confidence is downgraded (and thus hidden by default). It never
false-accuses a used import.

Source: /code-review max multi-agent review of the unused-imports PR
(PR #4, branch `index-correctness`) — finding #1.
design:
Preferred fix (at the resolver, benefits every consumer of a bare `crate`
module reference): make the empty-tail `crate` case return the crate's
entry-point file when it exists, mirroring the `head =>` workspace-crate arm.
i.e. a bare `crate` module reference resolves to `lib.rs`/`main.rs`, not the
`src/` directory. This likely needs the crate's `entry_point_file()` at the
resolution site, so `resolve_crate_path` (or its caller) may need the crate,
not just `crate_root`.

CAUTION — scope carefully (this is why it is its own PR): the `"crate"` arm and
`resolve_crate_path([])` are used widely (self/super sibling resolution,
`qualified_splits` prefix enumeration, `compute_dependencies`). Changing the
empty-path return value must be checked against all of them. The safest scoping
may be to only return the entry-point file on the import-module resolution path
(`resolve_import_segments`), leaving prefix enumeration untouched.

Narrower alternative (lower blast radius, but fixes only this symptom): handle
it in `classify_confidence` — when `import.path == ["crate"]`, look up the
imported name in the crate entry-point file directly instead of relying on the
resolver's directory result. Does not help any other consumer.

Related: interacts with the nested-group flattening issue (sibling) — both feed
a collapsed `["crate"]` path into the same resolver hole.
acceptance_criteria:
* `use crate::Foo;` for an unused crate-root `pub struct Foo` is reported as
  Definite (not MaybeTrait), with source_module "crate".
* A resolver-level unit test covers bare `crate` module resolution ->
  entry-point file (lib.rs/main.rs), not the src/ directory.
* No regression in cross-file resolution or compute_dependencies: full test
  suite green, and any behavior change to the shared `"crate"` arm is enumerated
  against its other callers (self/super, qualified_splits).

title: tethys: push name/Ca/Ce sort to SQL ORDER BY (keep instability in Rust)
description:
get_coupling_metrics (architecture.rs) fetches all rows from arch_coupling unsorted, then sorts in Rust. This was a deliberate choice for the Instability variant — the formula lives only in CouplingMetrics::instability() and can't be replicated in SQL without duplicating logic. For the other three sort variants (Name, Afferent, Efferent) the choice was made by consistency, not necessity.

Reviewer flagged in PR 60 round 7: "At scale (monorepos with hundreds of crates) the full fetch + in-memory sort could become noticeable... consider adding a follow-up issue for DB-side sorting of the non-instability variants (name, Ca, Ce) if performance ever becomes a concern."

Trigger condition: a workspace with hundreds of crates where get_coupling_metrics becomes a measurable hot path.

Scope:
- Add ORDER BY clauses to the query in get_coupling_metrics for Name/Ca/Ce variants
- Keep the Rust-side sort for Instability (formula stays in one place)
- The current secondary sort (by package name) for ties stays in the SQL ORDER BY

Note: SQLite COLLATE NOCASE may be wanted for the Name variant to match the locale-insensitive lexicographic sort Rust's String::cmp provides today.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] get_coupling_metrics emits ORDER BY for CouplingSort::Name, ::Afferent, ::Efferent
- [ ] Instability sort still happens in Rust (formula stays in one place)
- [ ] Tie-breaking by name remains deterministic across variants
- [ ] Benchmark before/after on a synthetic >200-crate workspace

title: Import-usage detection: a method call makes a same-named unused free-fn import look used (phantom file_dep)
description:
compute_dependencies (src/indexing.rs:896) decides an import is 'used' if the imported name appears in the file's referenced names (built from parsed ExtractedReference.name + path prefixes). It does not distinguish a free-function reference from a method call of the same name. So an UNUSED free-fn import collides with a same-named method and produces a phantom file dependency.

Repro:
  src/a.rs: pub fn process() {}
  src/b.rs:
    use crate::a::process;        // never called as a free fn -> unused
    pub struct T; impl T { pub fn process(&self) {} }
    pub fn run(t: &T) { t.process(); }   // method call, name 'process'
  After index: file_deps contains b.rs -> a.rs (PHANTOM; b does not use a::process).

Independent of tethys-6rlu (the refs_named view does not touch dependency computation, which runs in Pass 1 from parsed names before resolution). Same name-collision class as tethys-53iv but in the import-usage/file_deps layer rather than ref resolution. K-hybrid corroboration (file_deps) may already mitigate some cases; this fixture shows a residual.

Impact: over-reported file dependencies / coupling edges; false cycles possible.
acceptance_criteria:
* In the repro, no b.rs -> a.rs file_dep is produced (the import is correctly unused).
* A method call does not count as a use of a same-named free-function import.
* Legitimate import-usage detection (free-fn import actually called) unchanged (regression).

title: Incrementally add #[errors] doc to public Tethys methods
description: The entire impl Tethys block has #[expect(clippy::missing_errors_doc)]. As the API stabilizes, chip away at this by documenting errors on individual methods and narrowing the expect scope. Flagged in PR #55 review.

title: Resolver declines single-segment relative import paths: pub use inner::item resolves only when the name is workspace-unique
description:
Import-path resolution declines bare single-segment relative module paths (source_module='inner' from use inner::item within src/lib.rs) — documented in tests/idxperf_golden.rs as 'the resolver declines the bare single-segment util import path'. Refs whose name matches such an import then fall through to the unscoped unique-name fallback: they resolve when the name is workspace-unique and stay unresolved otherwise.

Surfaced by the tethys-v1w8 slice-3 collision fence: pub use inner::helper (the MOST idiomatic re-export shape — parent re-exporting a child module item by relative path) plus a same-named decoy fn in another file leaves BOTH the reexport ref and a same-file bare call unresolved, while a crate::inner::helper-anchored import resolves fine. Parity between reexport refs and bare usage holds exactly (v1w8 C2) — the gap is upstream in path resolution and affects both equally.

Impact: (a) callers/impact under-count for non-unique names imported via bare relative paths; (b) dead-code residual false positive: a re-exported symbol whose name is not workspace-unique and whose pub use is bare-relative keeps zero inbound refs even after v1w8.

Likely fix shape: resolve single-segment relative paths against the importing file's own module directory (src/lib.rs + 'inner' -> src/inner.rs; src/a/mod-file + 'inner' -> src/a/inner.rs), i.e. the same semantics rustc gives a use path's leading in-crate segment. Interacts with tethys-9z7i (the current unique-name fallback binding would carry a 'speculative' band; this fix upgrades the strategy to path-directed).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fixture: pub use inner::helper with a same-named decoy in another file resolves to inner.rs's helper (path-directed, not unique-fallback)
- [ ] Same fixture: bare body call via use inner::helper resolves identically (parity with v1w8 C2 fence)
- [ ] Existing anchored-path (crate::/self::) and multi-segment resolution unchanged
- [ ] tests/reexport_refs.rs collision fence expectations updated (it pins the decline today with a TRIPWIRE note)

title: C# parity for deprecated-callers: attribute extraction + [Obsolete] detection
description:
## Parent

tethys-l6nt (scope PRD) — Act 1, C# parity user story: both indexed languages get the same health signals.

## What to build

Extend the deprecated-callers analysis to C#. The blocker today is upstream of the analysis: symbol attribute extraction is Rust-only, so [Obsolete] is never recorded. This slice cuts through that layer end-to-end: extract attributes for C# symbols during parsing (at minimum Obsolete, stored the same way Rust attributes are), detect obsolete-marked symbols, and surface their call sites through the same CLI subcommand with identical table/JSON shape.

C# dependency resolution is deliberately more conservative than Rust (namespace/using corroboration), so caller lists may be narrower — that is acceptable and consistent with the error posture; do not loosen resolution to widen results.

## Blocked by

- tethys-jdly (deprecated-callers analysis, Rust) — this slice extends its command surface.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] C# fixture: call sites of an [Obsolete] method are listed by the same CLI subcommand
- [ ] C# attribute extraction stores at minimum the Obsolete attribute alongside symbols, matching the Rust storage shape
- [ ] Obsolete message/error flag surfaced when present
- [ ] JSON output shape identical across languages
- [ ] Rust deprecated-callers behavior unchanged (regression)
- [ ] Tests build their own index from C# fixture workspaces, never an ambient DB

title: refs table omits free-function identifiers used as values and macro-token-tree identifiers
description:
The Rust reference extractor produces refs ONLY for call_expression, struct_expression, and type_identifier nodes (src/languages/rust.rs:165-250). Two real categories of free-function/identifier references are therefore never recorded:

1. Function-as-value: a function referenced WITHOUT calling it — passed as a callback or stored (iter.map(node_text), let f = validate;). This is a plain identifier in argument/value position (not a call_expression, not a type_identifier), so no ref is emitted.
2. Macro token-tree identifiers: identifiers (including calls) that appear only inside a macro invocation's token tree are not parsed into call_expression nodes, so references that exist only at a macro call site are missed.

Impact: a function used ONLY as a value (never called via call_expression) has zero inbound call-refs/call_edges, so it is a FALSE POSITIVE for the dead-code finder (tethys-dvsw) and undercounted by hotspots (tethys-7p54) — which is why those depend on this issue.

Scope note: this is NOT the same as bare CALL foo(), which IS captured correctly (see closed tethys-zp2j). And it is distinct from the reference_name-nulling queryability bug (tethys-6rlu). Category 1 (fn-as-value) is the higher-value half; category 2 (macro tokens) is lower-value and higher false-positive — split if needed.

Provenance: 'Missing refs: fn-as-value and macro token-tree identifiers invisible' from the June 2026 indexing-strategy review; never previously filed (an earlier note cited a phantom ID tethys-cfme).
design: tree-sitter-rust: (1) fn-as-value is an identifier node in expression/argument position whose parent is NOT a call_expression callee and NOT a scoped_identifier — needs a textual/symbol-table guard to avoid recording every identifier as a ref. (2) macro identifiers live under token_tree inside macro_invocation; the extractor currently does not descend into token_tree. Both are higher-false-positive than zp2j's bare-call case, so apply conservative guards (suppressions, not accusations) per the project's error posture.
acceptance_criteria:
* fn-as-value: indexing a workspace where an in-crate free function foo is passed as a value (e.g. iter.map(foo) or let g = foo;) yields >= 1 refs row with reference_name='foo' (kind distinguishing value-use from call is acceptable).
* macro-token identifier: an explicit decision is recorded — either a refs row is produced for an in-crate symbol referenced only inside a macro token tree, or it is documented as out-of-scope with rationale.
* Existing bare-call (tethys-zp2j), method-call, and qualified-call extraction is unchanged (regression test).
* Conservative: no spurious refs for shadowed locals / non-symbol identifiers.
notes: Filed 2026-06-30 during the zp2j dedup review. Originally framed as a zp2j sibling; reframed to stand alone after zp2j was verified invalid and closed. fn-as-value gap confirmed by reading the extractor dispatch (no arm records a standalone identifier used as a value).

title: tethys: expose --depth flag on `callers --transitive` for parity with impact
description:
PR #57 wired `--depth` through `tethys impact`, but `tethys callers --transitive` still calls `get_symbol_impact(symbol, None)` unconditionally. Users will reasonably expect the same flag on both commands.

Add a `--depth` CLI flag on `callers` and pass it through to `get_symbol_impact`.

title: PR #60 polish backlog: cleanup items deferred from review rounds
description:
Collection of low-priority cleanup items surfaced during PR #60's third review round. Each is real but represents diminishing returns relative to the work already done; bundle them for a future hygiene pass.

**Items:**

1. Remove the now-redundant ''suggestions_capped_at_five'' test in cli/coupling.rs — the new ''suggestions_capped_at_five_returns_first_five_by_input_order'' is a strict superset. Both currently exist and assert the same length on identical inputs.

2. Add an ''add_file'' helper to ''package_coupling_tests'' (it already exists in ''repopulate_architecture_tests''). The new sort-order test in particular repeats the inline ''upsert_file'' pattern 7 times.

3. Strengthen ''get_package_coupling_returns_err_for_corrupt_target_source'': currently asserts only ''is_err()''. Add assertions that the error is the expected variant (''Error::Internal'') and that the message contains the corrupt source value (''totally-bogus'') so a future change that drops diagnostic context would fail the test.

4. Corrupt-source tests use hardcoded package ''id=1,2,3'' and rely on ''PRAGMA ignore_check_constraints'' silently succeeding. Add a verification ''SELECT'' after the corrupt INSERT to confirm the bad row landed, so a silent PRAGMA failure produces a clear diagnostic at the injection point instead of a confusing downstream assertion mismatch. Optionally switch the inserts to use ''INSERT ... RETURNING id'' or query back ids rather than hardcoding.

5. The ''completed_path_writes_nothing_to_stdout'' assertion message embeds the ''rivets-tuph'' ticket reference. Move it to a ''// TODO(rivets-tuph)'' comment above the assertion so the failure message stays clean and the editorial intent stays out of test output.

6. Tighten the ''#[expect(clippy::cast_precision_loss, reason = ...)]'' annotation on ''CouplingMetrics::instability'' to explicitly say ''the annotated cast is ; the numerator uses  which is infallible widening, exempt from the lint.''

7. Add a reciprocal cross-reference: ''Package.name'' field doc should point at ''PackageId'' (''recommended stable identity; see [] for why the numeric id is unsuitable for that purpose''). Currently the ''PackageId → name'' direction is documented but not the reverse.

8. The ''none_writes_nothing'' assertion is bare ''assert!(buf.is_empty())'' with no contract message. Add an explanation so a failure tells a future maintainer what the ''None'' arm represents.

Discovered during third-round PR #60 review (the 6 specialist reviewers ran on commits a4949bb..HEAD).

title: Discriminated NotFound variants in tethys::Error (e.g., PackageNotFound)
description:
Currently tethys::Error::NotFound(String) carries an ad-hoc payload format like 'package: foo', 'file: bar', 'symbol: baz'. The string content is a de facto protocol for distinguishing what was not found, but nothing in the type enforces or documents it.

This becomes a problem when rivets-mcp adds a tethys_coupling tool. If MCP needs to react differently to 'package not found' vs 'file not found' (e.g., suggest re-indexing for the former, suggest checking the path for the latter), it has to parse the string payload — a brittle implicit contract.

**Fix:** Introduce discriminated variants on tethys::Error:
- PackageNotFound(String)
- (existing NotFound(String) stays as catch-all for legacy callers)

Update get_package_coupling and cli/coupling.rs::run_detail_to to use PackageNotFound.

**Why deferred:** Low urgency today because (a) no MCP consumer of get_package_coupling exists yet, (b) the string format isn't being parsed anywhere, (c) main.rs renders both variants the same way. The right time to fix is just before rivets-o4re wires up tethys_coupling.

Discovered during the durability review pass on PR #60.

title: Interactive HTML graph export
description:
Generate a static HTML/CSS/JS bundle that visualizes the indexed graph for human exploration. Three files served from a local directory, no server required, works offline.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph export build / start commands.

**Output:**
- .rivets/index/export/index.html — single HTML file
- .rivets/index/export/app.css
- .rivets/index/export/app.js — bundled with the graph data inlined as a const

**Visualization features (in priority order):**
1. Force-directed layout with color-coded nodes by kind, edge labels by kind.
2. Click-to-focus: click a node to inspect its details (kind, file:line, signature, degree).
3. Search box: filter nodes by name / qualified name / file path.
4. Path mode: click two nodes to find and highlight the shortest path between them.
5. Legend with click-to-hide for node kinds and edge kinds.
6. Degree-threshold filter slider.
7. Cluster mode: group nodes by directory.

**Lower priority (v2):**
- PNG export of current view.
- Heat-map mode coloring nodes by file mtime.
- Analytics charts (top-N most connected, kind distribution, degree histogram).

**Library choice:**
- Cytoscape.js (mature, MIT, ~300KB minified) is the best fit — covers force-directed, custom rendering, click handlers, path-finding, and clustering out of the box.
- Alternative: D3-force directly (more code but no large dep).

**Why P4 (lower priority):**
This is mostly orthogonal to indexing and the AI-assistant story. It's polish for human exploration. Defer until the analytical features (hotspots, surprising, coupling, etc.) are in place — the export then becomes a natural human counterpart to those CLI tools.

**CLI:**
- tethys export build [--out DIR] [--include-contains]
- tethys export start [--out DIR]    (build + open in browser)
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] tethys export build produces a self-contained directory of html/css/js
- [ ] Force-directed layout with color-coded nodes
- [ ] Click-to-focus and node detail panel
- [ ] Search by name / qualified name / file
- [ ] Shortest-path mode (depends on path-query feature, but can fall back to BFS in JS)
- [ ] Works offline (no CDN; deps bundled)
- [ ] Documented in tethys README

title: tethys: replace Mutex<Connection> with a pool; collapse get_package_coupling round-trips
description:
get_package_coupling currently does 3 sequential DB round-trips:
  1. SELECT target package row from arch_coupling JOIN arch_packages
  2. SELECT outgoing neighbors via fetch_neighbors(OUTGOING_SQL)
  3. SELECT incoming neighbors via fetch_neighbors(INCOMING_SQL)

The reason 2 and 3 can't share the connection guard with 1 is the Mutex<Connection> in db/mod.rs is non-reentrant — fetch_neighbors re-acquires the lock internally, so the outer guard from step 1 must drop first. This was the mutex bug caught and documented during PR 60 development.

Reviewer flagged in PR 60 round 5: "If the Mutex<Connection> is ever replaced with a connection pool (e.g. r2d2 or a custom pool), this could be collapsed into fewer round-trips."

This is architecturally the right time to address it — the connection-pool migration would benefit other code paths too (concurrent queries from MCP tools, parallel architecture phase passes, etc.).

Scope:
1. Add a connection pool (r2d2-sqlite or similar) wrapping rusqlite::Connection
2. Either re-architect get_package_coupling to hold one connection across all three queries (single transaction, isolation guarantees) OR fold all three queries into one with UNION + JOIN
3. Remove the documented scope-block workaround in get_package_coupling
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] db/mod.rs uses a connection pool instead of Mutex<Connection>
- [ ] get_package_coupling no longer needs the explicit-drop scope block
- [ ] Benchmark before/after for get_package_coupling on a workspace of >50 crates
- [ ] All existing tests still pass; the mutex non-reentrancy test stays green
- [ ] Document the pool sizing rationale in db/mod.rs

title: tethys: snapshot tests for tethys coupling CLI output
description: PR 60 (per-crate coupling metrics) implements the runtime, but the design spec in docs/design/tethys-architecture-analysis.md calls for CLI-level snapshot tests against a fixture workspace covering: default output, --sort name/ca/ce/instability, --package with hit and miss, and --json shape. The runtime is covered by 566 unit/integration/property tests; what is missing is end-to-end output stability.
design: Add insta or similar snapshot crate as dev-dep, build a small two-or-three-crate fixture workspace under crates/tethys/tests/fixtures/, and capture stdout for each invocation. Stripping ANSI is unnecessary because writing to a Vec<u8> isn't a TTY and colored elides codes automatically.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Snapshot of default 'tethys coupling' output
- [ ] Snapshot per --sort variant (instability/ca/ce/name)
- [ ] Snapshot of --package <name> detail (text + json)
- [ ] Snapshot of --package <miss> showing suggestions on stderr
- [ ] CI fails on unintentional output drift

title: Add --lsp flag to CLI commands
description:
Add `--lsp` flag to: index, callers, impact

Behavior:
- If --lsp and LSP available: use LSP
- If --lsp and LSP missing: error and halt with helpful message
- No flag: tree-sitter only

Error message should include installation instructions for rust-analyzer.

title: Store imports during indexing (Rust + C#)
description:
During file indexing, extract import statements and store them in the imports table.

For Rust: parse `use` statements via existing `extract_use_statements()`
For C#: parse `using` directives via existing `extract_using_directives()`

Map both to the common imports table format.

title: Parse Cargo.toml to detect actual crate root
description:
The code currently hardcodes `workspace_root/src/` as the crate root in `lib.rs:1016` and `lib.rs:1443`. This breaks for:
- Workspaces with custom `[lib].path` in Cargo.toml
- Binary crates vs library crates
- Multi-crate workspaces

Should parse Cargo.toml to find the actual crate entry point.

title: Scope SQLite busy_timeout based on read vs write workload
description:
The current Index::open sets a 30-second busy_timeout for all operations. This was chosen to handle concurrent test parallelism (rivets-byie's architecture phase writes interact with tests sharing the rivets workspace DB).

**The concern:** if a user runs an interactive command like 'tethys coupling' while a background 'tethys index' is running, the read-side query could silently block for up to 30s before its first SQL operation succeeds. With WAL mode, true read queries don't block on writes, but the schema/pragma writes in Index::open itself can. The user sees no feedback — just a long pause.

**Possible fix shapes:**
- Set a short busy_timeout (500ms or 1s) by default and override it inside indexing operations only.
- Add an Index::open_read_only or similar that uses a shorter timeout for query-only commands.
- Surface a 'waiting for lock' message after 2-3s if the operation is still blocked.

**Why deferred:** the concurrent-interactive scenario is rare in practice (most users run one tethys command at a time). The 30s timeout is conservative but safe. Worth tuning if anyone reports a real UX issue.

Discovered during second-round PR #60 review.

title: Per-language display spelling for qualified names at query/output boundary
description: tethys stores qualified names with a canonical `::` separator for all languages (a cache format, decided in .separator-fix/spec.md decision #5). C# consumers see `AuthService::Login` in CLI/library output where C# syntax would be `AuthService.Login`. Add a display-formatting layer at the query/output boundary that re-spells qualified names per the symbol's language. Storage stays canonical `::`; this is presentation only. Deferred from the separator-fix loop (see .separator-fix/spec.md, probe findings in .separator-fix/probe-findings.md).

title: Cross-File Reference Resolution
description:
Enable tethys to resolve references across files, so `tethys callers` and `tethys impact` work for symbols defined in other files.

Two phases:
1. **Tree-sitter resolution (~85% accuracy)** - resolve via import paths ✅ COMPLETE
2. **LSP refinement (~98% accuracy)** - delegate ambiguous cases to rust-analyzer/csharp-ls

Design doc: docs/plans/2026-01-28-cross-file-resolution.md
notes:
## Phase 1 Status: COMPLETE (2026-01-28)

All Phase 1 tasks completed:
- ✅ imports table added to schema
- ✅ Import extraction for Rust (`use`) and C# (`using`)
- ✅ Unresolved references stored with symbol_id = NULL
- ✅ Pass 2 resolution implemented
- ✅ Integration tests passing
- ✅ Performance benchmarks documented in BENCHMARKS.md

## Phase 2 Status: IN PROGRESS

Completed tasks:
- ✅ rivets-8j9u: Add lsp-types dependency
- ✅ rivets-h1va: Implement LspClient with JSON-RPC transport
- ✅ rivets-nwwm: Implement LspProvider trait + RustAnalyzerProvider

Remaining tasks:
- rivets-oeu5: Add --lsp flag to CLI commands
- rivets-k3mv: Integrate LSP into index --lsp
- rivets-1dza: Integrate LSP into callers --lsp
- rivets-6x7g: Add CSharpLsProvider
- rivets-9o82: Add gated integration tests

Key commit: feat(tethys): add LSP client infrastructure for Phase 2

title: Compute module_path for symbols
description:
The schema has a `module_path` column (indexed) but symbols are stored with empty strings. See `lib.rs:624` and `lib.rs:753`.

The existing `resolve_module_path` in `resolver.rs` computes **file paths**, not **module paths** (like `crate::storage::issue`). Need to compute the actual Rust module path from the file location.

This would enable queries like "find all symbols in module X".

title: Integrate LSP into callers --lsp
description:
When `--lsp` flag is set for callers query:

1. Return stored references (from DB)
2. Additionally, ask LSP find_references for the symbol
3. Merge results, deduplicate

This catches callers that tree-sitter couldn't resolve during indexing.

title: Resolution NULLs refs.reference_name — name-based queries over refs are silently empty for resolved symbols
description:
Pass-2 resolution sets reference_name=NULL once a ref resolves to an in-crate symbol (src/db/references.rs:157: UPDATE refs SET symbol_id=?2, reference_name=NULL WHERE id=?1). Initial storage also only populates reference_name when symbol_id IS NULL (src/indexing.rs:779, src/batch_writer.rs:329).

Net effect: refs.reference_name is populated ONLY for UNRESOLVED references. Every reference that resolves to an in-crate symbol keeps just a numeric symbol_id and has reference_name=NULL.

Consequence (the real bug): name-based SQL over the refs table is silently incomplete. SELECT ... FROM refs WHERE reference_name='foo' returns 0 for any in-crate symbol foo that resolved — even though the ref rows exist. The only correct name query is a join: JOIN symbols s ON r.symbol_id=s.id WHERE s.name='foo'. External/ad-hoc SQL consumers have no way to know this and get vacuous results that look like 'no references'.

This is the actual root cause behind the now-closed tethys-zp2j: a kiro-control-center plan-lint gate filtering refs.reference_name='make_service' found the refs 'vacuous' and fell back to the imports table. The refs were present (resolved, reference_name NULL); the gate measured the wrong column.

Verified (self-index): bare free fn node_text => 0 rows WHERE reference_name='node_text', but 60 refs + 37 call_edges via JOIN symbols on symbol_id (kind='call'). Globally: 1884 resolved call-refs carry reference_name=NULL; 7673 unresolved carry a name.
design:
DECISION (falsifiable-design 2026-06-30): add an additive SQL view 'refs_named' (LEFT JOIN refs->symbols, COALESCE(reference_name, symbols.name) AS name). NOT option 1 (overload reference_name).

Rationale (subtractive sweep): overloading reference_name to always carry the name breaks two consumers that assume 'reference_name populated => unresolved': panic_points.rs:51,103 (WHERE reference_name IN ('unwrap','expect') with no symbol_id guard -> in-crate unwrap/expect gain false panic sites) and indexing.rs:1003 compute_dependencies_from_stored (resolved same-file names leak into refs_set -> phantom file_deps). The view leaves reference_name semantics untouched (no consumer changes) and computes name live (no rename staleness).

Probe+oracle agreed (5 fns, counts 1-59). Cheapest falsifier passed against the real view on a fresh index. Full design + 7-claim falsification table: .tethys-6rlu/design.md. Implementation: add CREATE VIEW IF NOT EXISTS refs_named to src/db/schema.rs; document refs_named as the name-query surface.

NOTE: on-disk index was stale during probing -> every regression fence must build its OWN index from a fixture, never query an ambient DB.
acceptance_criteria:
* After indexing + Pass-2 resolution, a ref to an in-crate symbol foo is retrievable by name from refs WITHOUT manually joining symbols (reference_name stays populated, or a documented view/column provides it).
* A regression fixture pins the node_text-style case: name-query count over refs == symbol_id-join count for a known in-crate free function.
* Storage decision (denormalize vs view) recorded in the issue.
* Existing resolved-ref consumers (get_callers, call_edges, dead-code) unaffected; no double-counting.
notes: Closed: Implemented on feat/tethys-6rlu-refs-named: additive refs_named SQL view (COALESCE(reference_name, symbols.name) over LEFT JOIN), auto-applied on every Index::open. 7 regression fences (C1-C7); full suite green, clippy clean, prove-it oracle 5/5. C1 narrowed to resolved+bare-unresolved (unresolved qualified refs keyed by qualified path — tethys-3i35); C5 folded into the root invariant. reference_name semantics untouched -> no consumer change.

title: tethys: test format_uri returns Ok for nonexistent path (I/O-free seam)
description:
The path_to_uri / format_uri split exposes a contract that format_uri is I/O-free (its rustdoc says 'without performing any filesystem I/O'). The existing test path_to_uri_returns_invalid_path_for_nonexistent only verifies the wrapper still surfaces canonicalize errors — it doesn't pin down the seam itself.

Add a test asserting format_uri(Path::new(r'C:\does\not\exist\foo.rs')) returns Ok. If someone later 'fixes' format_uri by re-introducing a metadata() or canonicalize() call, that test catches the regression and forces them to explain.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (pr-test-analyzer).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Test added to crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs#tests
- [ ] Asserts format_uri returns Ok for a deliberately-nonexistent path
- [ ] cfg-gated appropriately for Windows/Unix path shapes
notes: Closed: Consolidated into tethys-w3z7 (transport.rs format_uri hardening bundle); full details preserved here.

title: Add reference kind tracking to call_edges table
description:
The `call_edges` table currently only tracks caller→callee relationships with call counts, but loses information about the **type** of reference (call, import, type usage, etc.).

**Current limitation:**
- `get_callers` returns `reference_kinds: vec![ReferenceKind::Call]` as a hardcoded default
- The original join-based query used `GROUP_CONCAT(kind)` to aggregate actual reference kinds
- The `parse_reference_kinds` helper function was removed as dead code

**Why this matters:**
- Impact analysis may want to distinguish "function calls" from "type references"
- Security audits may care about data flow (calls) vs type dependencies (imports)
- Callers could filter by reference kind for more targeted queries

**Proposed enhancement:**
```sql
ALTER TABLE call_edges ADD COLUMN kinds TEXT;
-- Store as comma-separated or JSON array: "call,call,import"
```

Or normalize with a separate table:
```sql
CREATE TABLE call_edge_kinds (
    caller_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    callee_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    kind TEXT NOT NULL,
    count INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
    PRIMARY KEY (caller_symbol_id, callee_symbol_id, kind)
);
```

**Implementation notes:**
- Update `populate_call_edges` SQL to GROUP BY kind as well
- Restore `parse_reference_kinds` function or use JSON
- Update `get_callers`/`get_callees` to return actual kinds

**Trade-off:**
- More storage and slightly more complex queries
- But provides richer information for analysis

title: Pass 2: self-crate qualified call crate::X() does not resolve to in-crate X
description:
A qualified call through the crate root, crate::helper(), to an in-crate free function helper (defined in the crate root lib.rs) is NOT resolved in Pass 2: the ref stays unresolved (symbol_id NULL) with reference_name='crate::helper'.

Contrast (both work): a BARE helper() via 'use crate::helper;' resolves; and a submodule-qualified helper::do_thing_q() resolves (see tests/pass2_qualified_paths.rs::submodule_qualified_call_resolves). So the gap is specifically the leading 'crate' segment / crate-root absolute path — resolve.rs::qualified_module_fallback + resolver::resolve_module_path do not map 'crate' to the crate root.

Repro:
  src/lib.rs:  pub mod b; pub fn helper() {}
  src/b.rs:    use crate::helper; pub fn use_it() { helper(); crate::helper(); }
  After index:
    SELECT symbol_id, reference_name FROM refs r JOIN files f ON r.file_id=f.id
      WHERE f.path LIKE '%b.rs';
    -> bare helper() : symbol_id set (resolved)
    -> crate::helper(): symbol_id NULL, reference_name='crate::helper' (UNRESOLVED)

Impact: callers/impact/reachability under-count symbols invoked via crate:: paths. Also surfaced in tethys-6rlu: the refs_named view keys this unresolved ref by 'crate::helper', so a bare-name query (WHERE name='helper') misses it. tethys-6rlu documents that as a limitation; resolving crate:: here would remove it (the view then keys the ref by symbols.name='helper', no view change needed).
acceptance_criteria:
* After indexing the repro, the crate::helper() ref resolves: symbol_id points to the in-crate helper symbol and reference_name is NULL.
* tethys callers helper lists the crate::helper() call site.
* Existing submodule-qualified and bare-call resolution unchanged (regression).
* A fixture pins crate::X() (X in crate root) resolving.

title: Implement LspProvider trait + RustAnalyzerProvider
Architecture analysis: package detection and coupling metrics (Ca/Ce/instability)
description:
Detect high-level project structure (packages and architectural layers) and compute coupling metrics between them. Useful for refactor planning, codebase health monitoring, and PR review.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's architecture/coupling/package commands. The metrics (afferent coupling, efferent coupling, instability) are Robert C. Martin classics — well-established and immediately legible to seasoned engineers.

**What we already have:**
- Cargo.toml discovery via cargo::discover_crates → CrateInfo with name + path.
- file_deps table with from_file_id → to_file_id edges.
- Both feed naturally into per-crate rollups; no new parsing required.

**What this adds:**
- arch_packages table: id, name, path, source ('manifest'|'directory'), language, version, manifest_path.
- arch_file_packages: file_id → package_id mapping.
- arch_package_deps: package_id → package_id with import_count, rolled up from file_deps.
- arch_coupling: per-package Ca, Ce, instability = Ce / (Ca + Ce).

Definitions:
- Ca (afferent coupling): how many other packages depend on this one. Higher = more stable / load-bearing.
- Ce (efferent coupling): how many packages this one depends on. Higher = more unstable.
- Instability: 0 = maximally stable (everyone depends on it, it depends on nothing); 1 = maximally unstable.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::get_packages() -> Vec<Package>
- Tethys::get_coupling_metrics(package_name: Option<&str>) -> Vec<CouplingEntry>
- Tethys::get_package_dependencies(package_id) -> Vec<PackageDep>

**CLI:**
- tethys architecture [--format table|json]
- tethys coupling [--package NAME] [--sort instability|ca|ce|name]
- tethys package <NAME> [--no-files]

**Layer detection (lower priority, follow-up):**
KiroGraph also classifies files into layers (api/service/data/ui/shared) by path patterns. Could be a v2; the package-level metrics are the high-value primary feature.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Schema migration adds arch_packages, arch_file_packages, arch_package_deps, arch_coupling
- [ ] Architecture analysis runs as a phase during indexing (only when enabled — opt-in flag)
- [ ] CLI: tethys architecture, tethys coupling, tethys package
- [ ] Coupling metrics correctly compute Ca, Ce, instability for the rivets workspace itself
- [ ] MCP tool tethys_coupling and tethys_architecture (sibling rivets-o4re)
- [ ] Documented in tethys README
- [ ] Tests verify metrics on a multi-crate fixture workspace

description:

Run with: `cargo test --ignored`

title: tethys: track parent_symbol_id for nested symbols

notes: Closed: Duplicate of tethys-aay4 (same parent_symbol_id-always-None gap; tethys-fe4f was previously closed as a dup of aay4).
